summary_text:
S:
The patient reports intermittent numbness and tingling in both hands for the past few weeks, beginning after a car accident last December. She notes worsening symptoms over the last month and reports mild neck stiffness following the accident. She describes the tingling as most prominent in the right hand, extending from the middle and ring fingers to the forearm, and more pronounced in the left hand with the pinky and ring fingers. She reports occasional throbbing pain, but overall feels weaker, particularly when using her hands. She experiences this primarily at night and throughout the day. She denies any loss of sensation in general, but feels less sensitive than expected. She has not been consistently taking nerve pain medication. The patient reports fatigue and weight gain as recent changes. She also reports excessive appetite and bloating. She notes floaters in her vision and reports shortness of breath and leg swelling. She states she is always hungry.

O:
Physical exam reveals diminished pinprick sensation in the third and fourth fingers of the right hand and the fourth and fifth fingers of the left hand. Reflexes are one plus and symmetric at the upper extremities and one plus and symmetric at the lower extremities. Tinel's signs are positive at both wrists and both elbows. Neurological exam is otherwise unremarkable. Blood pressure is mildly low. Heart rate is within normal limits.

A:
Bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome is suspected. Cubital tunnel syndrome cannot be ruled out. The patient's symptoms are consistent with peripheral neuropathy.

P:
Schedule an EMG and NCV study. If symptoms do not resolve, consider referral to hand clinic for possible carpal tunnel release surgery or symptomatic injections. Encourage consistent splint use. Follow up on EMG/NCV results. Counsel the patient on the importance of adherence to her current medications, including carvedilol, lexapro, and addressing her pre-diabetes and anxiety.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Upper extremity tingling

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

The patient is a 60-year-old female referred today for evaluation of numbness and tingling.

The patient complains of numbness, tingling, and pain in both hands which began last December after she broke both wrists in an MVA and which has progressively worsened since then. She continues to wear a splint on the right wrist all the time and on the left only intermittently, which she states is because the right wrist was injured more severely in the MVA. The sensation radiates from the hands into the forearms and is equal bilaterally. The 4th and 5th fingers in the left hand and 3rd and 4th on the right-hand tingle. She states that her fingers are sore with palpation and seem weaker than before. Any exertion exacerbates the tingling, and after something exacerbates the pain, the tingling seems "dull". The symptoms come and go and seem to occur mostly at night. She denies complete loss of sensation in any of her fingers, but states that they seem less sensitive than they should be. she reports mild stiffness in her neck. She notes that she did hit her head during the MVA and was diagnosed with a contusion. She states that she has been given "nerve pain medicine” but has not been taking it.

The patient takes carvedilol for her hypertension and Lexapro for her anxiety. She reports a history of prediabetes, which she has brought under control via dietary changes. She denies any history of thyroid issues.

REVIEW OF SYMPTOMS

• Constitutional Symptoms: The patient reports increased fatigue and weight gain.
• Eyes: Positive for floaters.
• Cardiovascular: Positive for leg swelling.
• Respiratory: Positive for shortness of breath.
• Gastrointestinal: Positive for excessive hunger and bloating.
• Musculoskeletal: Positive for gout and muscle pain.
• Integumentary: Positive for nail bed changes, dryness, and itching.
• Neurological: Positive for bilateral paresthesia of hands.
• Endocrine: Positive prediabetes and excessive hunger.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

Chronic kidney disease (CKD).
Prediabetes, diet controlled.
Hypertension.
Gastric esophageal reflux disorder (GERD)

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Carvedilol 25 mg twice daily.
Lexapro 10 mg daily.

ALLERGIES

Sulfa, causes rash.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Neurological Exam
Sensation is normal except pinprick sensation diminished in the 3rd and 4th fingers on the right and the 4th and 5th fingers on the left. 1+ and symmetric, 2+ knees. Toes are downgoing bilaterally. Tinel's sign positive at both wrists and both elbows.

RESULTS

CT brain with and without contrast, 02/05/2021
Impression: No metastatic disease. No acute intracranial abnormalities. I personally reviewed the report and images.

ASSESSMENT

• Bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome, question of bilateral cubital tunnel syndrome.

PLAN

I discussed my impression with the patient. I will schedule her for EMG/NCV. If her symptoms do not resolve, we can consider Hand Clinic referral for possibly symptomatic injections or carpal tunnel release surgery, although she can also pursue these through her current orthopedist. I encouraged her to wear her splints.

INSTRUCTIONS

Return for EMG/NCV.

section_miscategorizations.text: Counsel the patient on the importance of adherence to her current medications, including carvedilol, lexapro, and addressing her pre-diabetes and anxiety.
section_miscategorizations.reason: The counseling action appropriately belongs in the Plan, but the specific listing of current medications (carvedilol, lexapro) and existing conditions (pre-diabetes, anxiety) is partly serving as current-medication/problem documentation, which by template rules would more naturally appear in S or A unless tied directly to a new change. Here it is mixed into the counseling statement, so the placement is acceptable but slightly organizationally blurred.
